#html:true
#tags column:3

#notetype:_Cloze - DecEyeStrain
Depolarising NMBAs are {{c1::<u>agonists</u>}} at nicotinic ACh receptors → {{c2::<u>persistent depolarisation</u>}} → paralysis. Initial receptor activation causes {{c3::<u>fasciculations</u>}}.
Non-depolarising NMBAs are {{c1::<u>competitive antagonists</u>}} at nicotinic ACh receptors → prevent depolarisation, with {{c2::<u>no initial activation</u>}} (unlike depolarising agents).
Suxamethonium: onset ~{{c1::60 seconds}}
Suxamethonium is given by {{c1::IV bolus only}} — not as an infusion.
Suxamethonium causes a transient {{c1::↑ serum K⁺}} due to initial nicotinic receptor activation → depolarisation of muscle membranes.
Suxamethonium is safe within the first {{c1::24 hours}} post-injury, before significant nicotinic ACh receptor {{c2::upregulation}} occurs.

In burns, denervation, and neuromuscular disease, suxamethonium is contraindicated because upregulated ACh receptors → {{c1::massive K⁺ release}} → potentially fatal {{c2::arrhythmias}}. Due to ACh receptor upregulation, not the normal transient K+ rise.
Non-depolarising NMBAs are classified structurally as {{c1::<u>aminosteroids</u>}} (rocuronium, vecuronium, pancuronium) or {{c2::<u>benzylisoquinoliniums</u>}} (atracurium, cisatracurium).
Rocuronium has the {{c1::fastest onset}} of non-depolarising agents and is used as an alternative to suxamethonium for {{c2::RSI}}.
Rocuronium: onset {{c1::1–3 minutes}}, duration {{c2::30–90 minutes}}.
Rocuronium doses: bolus {{c1::0.6 mg/kg IV}}, infusion {{c2::0.6–0.9 mg/kg/hr}}.
Vecuronium is approximately {{c1::6×}} more potent than rocuronium and has a {{c2::shorter}} duration.
Vecuronium: onset {{c1::3–4 minutes}}, duration {{c2::35–45 minutes}}.
Vecuronium doses: bolus {{c1::0.1 mg/kg IV}}, infusion {{c2::0.05–0.1 mg/kg/hr}}.
Atracurium undergoes {{c1::Hofmann elimination}} — organ-independent metabolism making it suitable in {{c2::liver and renal failure}}.
Atracurium's Hofmann elimination is affected by {{c1::temperature}} and {{c2::pH}}. Hypothermia and acidosis slow elimination.
Atracurium causes {{c1::histamine release}} → hypotension, tachycardia, flushing. Direct pharmacological effect — not anaphylaxis.
Atracurium: onset {{c1::3–4 minutes}}, duration {{c2::35–45 minutes}}.
Cisatracurium is an {{c1::isomer of atracurium}} with the same organ-independent (Hofmann) metabolism but {{c2::minimal histamine release}}. Hence preferred over atracurium.
Cisatracurium: onset {{c1::5–7 minutes}}, duration {{c2::35–45 minutes}}.
Cisatracurium doses: bolus {{c1::0.15 mg/kg IV}}, infusion {{c2::0.15–0.2 mg/kg/hr}}.
The preferred NMBA for {{c1::<u>ICU infusions</u>}} (e.g. ARDS) is {{c2::<u>cisatracurium</u>}} — organ-independent metabolism and minimal histamine release.
Pancuronium is a {{c1::long-acting}} aminosteroid NMBA — duration {{c2::60–120 minutes}}. Less commonly used due to prolonged effect.
Pancuronium: onset {{c1::2–4 minutes}}, duration {{c2::60–120 minutes}}.
Pancuronium doses: bolus {{c1::0.04–0.1 mg/kg IV}}, infusion {{c2::0.06–0.1 mg/kg/hr}}.
Pancuronium causes {{c1::tachycardia}} due to {{c2::vagolytic (vagotonic blockade)}} activity. Haemodynamically significant — note in cardiac patients.
